name: debugging
context: Debug HTTP requests with curl — verbose output, response timing, protocol traces, and diagnosing common failures
info: This context node covers how agents diagnose HTTP issues using curl's built-in debugging capabilities: verbose mode (-v) for seeing the full request/response exchange, the write-out format (-w) for extracting timing and metadata, trace modes (--trace, --trace-ascii) for protocol-level inspection, and systematic approaches to common failure categories (DNS resolution, TLS handshake, authentication, timeouts, redirect loops, and server errors). Agents consult this node when a curl request returns an unexpected result, a non-zero exit code, or an HTTP error status. The debugging workflow starts with -v to see what's happening, narrows with -w for specific metrics, and escalates to --trace for byte-level protocol inspection. See [[curl/request]] for request construction and [[curl/auth]] for authentication-specific debugging.
triggers: why is my curl request failing
body:
# Debugging with curl

When an HTTP request fails or returns unexpected results, curl provides layered debugging tools — from a quick verbose flag to full protocol traces. This guide covers the debugging workflow agents should follow.

## Debugging Workflow

1. **Add `-v`** to see the full request/response exchange
2. **Check the exit code** (`$?`) against the exit code table in [[curl]]
3. **Use `-w`** to extract specific timing and metadata
4. **Escalate to `--trace`** for byte-level protocol inspection
5. **Isolate the layer** — DNS, TCP, TLS, HTTP, or application

## Verbose Mode (-v)

The single most useful debugging flag. Shows DNS resolution, TCP connection, TLS handshake, request headers, and response headers.

```bash
curl -v https://api.example.com/users
```

Output lines prefixed with:
- `*` — curl internal info (connection, TLS, timing)
- `>` — data sent to server (request headers)
- `<` — data received from server (response headers)

```
* Trying 93.184.216.34:443...
* Connected to api.example.com (93.184.216.34) port 443
* SSL connection using TLSv1.3 / TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
> GET /users HTTP/2
> Host: api.example.com
> Authorization: Bearer eyJhbG...
> Accept: */*
>
< HTTP/2 200
< content-type: application/json
< x-request-id: abc-123
<
{"users": [...]}
```

Send verbose output to a file (keep stdout clean for the response body):
```bash
curl -v https://api.example.com/users 2>debug.log
```

## Write-Out Format (-w)

Extract specific response metadata without parsing verbose output.

```bash
# Status code
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://api.example.com/health

# Full timing breakdown
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '
DNS lookup:    %{time_namelookup}s
TCP connect:   %{time_connect}s
TLS handshake: %{time_appconnect}s
First byte:    %{time_starttransfer}s
Total time:    %{time_total}s
Download size: %{size_download} bytes
HTTP status:   %{http_code}
' https://api.example.com/data

# JSON output for programmatic use
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '{"status":%{http_code},"time":%{time_total},"size":%{size_download}}\n' \
     https://api.example.com/data

# Read format from a file
echo -n '%{http_code} %{time_total}s %{size_download}B' > curl-format.txt
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '@curl-format.txt' https://api.example.com/data
```

### Key Write-Out Variables

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `%{http_code}` | HTTP response status code |
| `%{time_namelookup}` | Time from start to DNS resolution complete |
| `%{time_connect}` | Time from start to TCP connection established |
| `%{time_appconnect}` | Time from start to TLS handshake complete |
| `%{time_pretransfer}` | Time from start to just before transfer begins |
| `%{time_starttransfer}` | Time from start to first response byte (TTFB) |
| `%{time_total}` | Total time for the operation |
| `%{time_redirect}` | Total time spent in redirects |
| `%{size_download}` | Total bytes received |
| `%{size_upload}` | Total bytes sent |
| `%{speed_download}` | Average download speed (bytes/sec) |
| `%{speed_upload}` | Average upload speed (bytes/sec) |
| `%{content_type}` | Content-Type of the response |
| `%{num_redirects}` | Number of redirects followed |
| `%{redirect_url}` | URL of the redirect target (when not following) |
| `%{ssl_verify_result}` | SSL verification result (0 = OK) |
| `%{remote_ip}` | IP address of the remote server |
| `%{remote_port}` | Port of the remote server |
| `%{local_ip}` | Local IP address used |
| `%{local_port}` | Local port used |

## Protocol Trace

For byte-level inspection when -v isn't enough.

```bash
# Full hex+ascii trace
curl --trace trace.log https://api.example.com/data

# ASCII-only trace (more readable)
curl --trace-ascii trace.log https://api.example.com/data

# Trace with timestamps
curl --trace trace.log --trace-time https://api.example.com/data
```

## Diagnosing Common Failures

### DNS Resolution (exit code 6)
```bash
# Check if the hostname resolves
curl -v https://api.example.com 2>&1 | grep 'Trying'

# Compare with direct DNS lookup
nslookup api.example.com
dig api.example.com

# Try with a specific DNS server
curl --dns-servers 8.8.8.8 https://api.example.com
```

### Connection Refused (exit code 7)
```bash
# Check if the port is open
curl -v --connect-timeout 5 https://api.example.com 2>&1 | grep -E 'Trying|Connected|refused'

# Try a different port
curl https://api.example.com:8443/resource

# Check if the service is listening (from the server)
ss -tlnp | grep ':443'
```

### TLS/SSL Errors (exit code 35, 51, 60)
```bash
# See the full TLS handshake
curl -v https://api.example.com 2>&1 | grep -E 'SSL|TLS|certificate'

# Check the server certificate
curl -vI https://api.example.com 2>&1 | grep -A5 'Server certificate'

# Skip verification (development only!)
curl -k https://self-signed.example.com

# Use a custom CA bundle
curl --cacert /path/to/ca-bundle.pem https://internal.example.com

# Check certificate expiration
echo | openssl s_client -connect api.example.com:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates
```

### Timeout (exit code 28)
```bash
# Identify where the time is spent
curl -s -o /dev/null -w 'DNS: %{time_namelookup}s\nConnect: %{time_connect}s\nTLS: %{time_appconnect}s\nTTFB: %{time_starttransfer}s\nTotal: %{time_total}s\n' \
     -m 10 https://api.example.com/slow

# Separate connection timeout from transfer timeout
curl --connect-timeout 5 -m 30 https://api.example.com/slow
```

### HTTP Errors (4xx, 5xx)
```bash
# See response body with error details
curl -s -w '\nHTTP %{http_code}\n' https://api.example.com/resource

# 401 Unauthorized — check auth headers sent
curl -v -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" https://api.example.com/resource 2>&1 | grep -E '^> Authorization|^< HTTP|^< www-authenticate'

# 403 Forbidden — check permissions, IP restrictions
curl -v https://api.example.com/admin 2>&1 | grep -E '^< HTTP|^< x-|^< www-authenticate'

# 404 Not Found — verify the URL path
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code} %{url_effective}\n' -L https://api.example.com/resouce

# 429 Too Many Requests — check rate limit headers
curl -v https://api.example.com/resource 2>&1 | grep -iE 'rate|limit|retry|x-ratelimit'

# 500+ Server Error — log full response for support
curl -D - -s https://api.example.com/resource > error-response.txt
```

### Redirect Loops
```bash
# See redirect chain
curl -v -L --max-redirs 10 https://example.com 2>&1 | grep -E '^< HTTP|^< location'

# Don't follow redirects — just show where it goes
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code} %{redirect_url}\n' https://short.url/abc
```

### Empty Response (exit code 52)
```bash
# Check if the server closes the connection immediately
curl -v https://api.example.com/resource 2>&1 | tail -20

# Try HTTP/1.1 instead of HTTP/2
curl --http1.1 -v https://api.example.com/resource
```

## Benchmarking

```bash
# Time a single request
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{time_total}s\n' https://api.example.com/data

# Multiple runs with average (using a loop)
for i in {1..10}; do
  curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{time_total}\n' https://api.example.com/data
done | awk '{sum+=$1; n++} END {printf "avg: %.3fs over %d requests\n", sum/n, n}'

# Timing breakdown for latency analysis
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '
  namelookup:  %{time_namelookup}s
  connect:     %{time_connect}s
  appconnect:  %{time_appconnect}s
  pretransfer: %{time_pretransfer}s
  starttransfer: %{time_starttransfer}s
  total:       %{time_total}s
  size:        %{size_download} bytes
' https://api.example.com/data
```

sections.title: Verbose mode (-v)
sections.body: Add `-v` to any curl command to see the full request/response cycle. Output goes to stderr, so the response body still goes to stdout. Lines starting with `*` are curl internal messages (connection info, TLS details). Lines starting with `>` are request headers sent to the server. Lines starting with `<` are response headers received. Redirect verbose to a file with `2>debug.log` to keep stdout clean for piping. This is always the first step when debugging.

sections.title: Write-out format strings (-w)
sections.body: The `-w` flag outputs formatted metadata after the transfer. Combine with `-s -o /dev/null` to suppress the body and progress meter. Key variables: `%{http_code}`, `%{time_total}`, `%{time_namelookup}`, `%{time_connect}`, `%{time_appconnect}`, `%{time_starttransfer}`, `%{size_download}`, `%{content_type}`, `%{remote_ip}`, `%{ssl_verify_result}`. Format strings can be loaded from a file with `-w @format.txt`. Output JSON with `-w '{"status":%{http_code},"time":%{time_total}}'` for programmatic consumption.

sections.title: Protocol tracing
sections.body: When `-v` isn't detailed enough, use `--trace file` for full hex+ASCII dumps of everything sent and received, or `--trace-ascii file` for ASCII-only output (more readable for HTTP). Add `--trace-time` to include timestamps on each trace line. This shows the exact bytes on the wire, including TLS negotiation, HTTP framing, and chunked transfer encoding. Trace files can be large — use only for targeted debugging.

sections.title: Diagnosing DNS and TLS failures
sections.body: **DNS (exit code 6)**: curl cannot resolve the hostname. Verify with `nslookup` or `dig`. Check `/etc/resolv.conf` for DNS server config. Try `--dns-servers 8.8.8.8` to bypass local DNS. **TLS (exit codes 35, 51, 60)**: SSL handshake or certificate verification failed. Use `-v` to see the TLS version, cipher, and certificate chain. Common fixes: `--cacert` for custom CA bundles (corporate environments), `-k` for self-signed certs (dev only!), check certificate expiration with `openssl s_client`. Exit code 35 is handshake failure (protocol mismatch), 51 is certificate name mismatch, 60 is untrusted CA.

sections.title: Diagnosing HTTP error responses
sections.body: **401 Unauthorized**: check that the `Authorization` header is being sent correctly (`-v | grep Authorization`). Verify the token hasn't expired. Check the `WWW-Authenticate` response header for the expected auth scheme. **403 Forbidden**: correct auth but insufficient permissions, IP restrictions, or CORS preflight failures. **404 Not Found**: verify the URL path — typos are common. Check if the API requires a trailing slash or specific path version. **429 Too Many Requests**: check `Retry-After`, `X-RateLimit-*` headers. Back off and retry. **5xx Server Error**: save the full response (`-D - -s URL > error.txt`) to send to the API provider. Try again after a delay.

sections.title: Timing and benchmarking
sections.body: Use `-w` with timing variables to measure request performance. The timing phases are: `time_namelookup` (DNS), `time_connect` (TCP), `time_appconnect` (TLS), `time_pretransfer` (protocol negotiation), `time_starttransfer` (TTFB — time to first byte), `time_total` (complete transfer). To identify bottlenecks: high `time_namelookup` = slow DNS, high `time_connect - time_namelookup` = network latency, high `time_appconnect - time_connect` = slow TLS, high `time_starttransfer - time_appconnect` = slow server processing. Run multiple iterations in a Bash loop and average with `awk` for reliable measurements.
